_source: TH_EXPLORE_IT_DATA.json (authoritative structured data). Real accessions, per-item licence + open_access flag. Nothing authored by an assistant.

station.tagline: She was born to ransom a king — and she never stopped being expensive.

museum_objects.title: Fastrada Coin of Charlemagne
museum_objects.description: A silver denier bearing the name of Fastrada, Charlemagne's third wife, acquired by the Centre Charlemagne in 2022.
museum_objects.significance: A coin minted for a queen who died within the year — dated so precisely by her death that we know almost the exact week Isabelle's story begins.
museum_objects.plain_language: A coin naming Charlemagne's wife Fastrada, who died the same year it was made — this tiny coin pins down the exact birth moment of French money.

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

museum_objects.title: Charlemagne Portrait Denarius (Temple Type)
museum_objects.description: An extremely rare silver coin showing Charlemagne in classical portrait style, only about 50 known to survive.
museum_objects.significance: Charlemagne wanted to look like a Roman emperor on his own money — proof that even the most powerful man in Europe still needed a Roman stamp of legitimacy.
museum_objects.plain_language: One of only about 50 surviving coins that actually show what Charlemagne looked like, styled to resemble ancient Roman emperors.

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

museum_objects.rights_note: NO OPEN IMAGE FOUND — panel must show text/citation only.

museum_objects.title: Carolingian Denier (Mainz Mint)
museum_objects.description: A unique surviving silver denier from the Mainz mint, part of the Bibliothèque nationale de France's medieval coin collection.
museum_objects.significance: One of a kind — literally the only one known to survive from its mint and date, a single silver witness to an entire monetary system.
museum_objects.plain_language: A silver coin that is the only known surviving example of its kind — one coin representing an entire vanished system of money.

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

museum_objects.rights_note: NO OPEN IMAGE FOUND — panel must show text/citation only.

museum_objects.title: Aachen Penny of Charlemagne
museum_objects.description: A Carolingian penny found through archaeological excavation in 2008, part of the standardized reform coinage.
museum_objects.significance: Dug out of the ground centuries later, still legible — Charlemagne's reform was built to last, and this coin is the proof.
museum_objects.plain_language: A silver penny dug up by archaeologists, part of the currency reform that gave medieval Europe its first standardized money.

resolved.warning: TERTIARY SOURCE — acceptable as a pointer, NOT as TH's evidence. Replace with the primary source it cites.

museum_objects.rights_note: CITED: the original object (rights unclear/blocked). DISPLAYED: an openly-licensed image of the same kind of object, credited to its own institution. We cite one and show the other, and we say so.

museum_objects.title: KAROLUS Monogram Denier
museum_objects.description: A silver denier bearing Charlemagne's monogram, struck across France, Germany, Northern Italy, and Northeast Spain.
museum_objects.significance: The same stamped symbol, accepted from Barcelona to the Rhine — this is the coin that first made 'French money' mean something across borders.
museum_objects.plain_language: A coin stamped with Charlemagne's personal symbol, accepted across a huge stretch of medieval Europe as if it were one currency zone.

primary_sources.title: Capitulary of Aix-la-Chapelle on the Value of Commodities
primary_sources.author: Charlemagne's court
primary_sources.description: Royal decree fixing prices and establishing that 12 denarii equal 1 solidus.
primary_sources.plain_language: An actual royal law from 797 AD that fixed prices and set the exchange rate between coin denominations.

primary_sources.title: Capitulary of Aix-la-Chapelle Concerning Adulterers of Money
primary_sources.author: Louis the Pious's court
primary_sources.description: Law imposing severe punishment — loss of a hand — for counterfeiters.
primary_sources.plain_language: A brutal medieval law: counterfeit the currency, and you lose a hand.

world_map.map_description: Isabelle's franc began as Charlemagne's silver denier reform in Aachen and traveled through royal écus and gold louis d'or before becoming the modern French franc.

nodes.label: Aachen (Aix-la-Chapelle)
nodes.description: Charlemagne's capital, where the livre-sou-denier monetary reform was decreed and much of the coinage was minted.

nodes.label: Mainz Mint
nodes.description: One of the key mints producing Carolingian silver deniers under the reform.

nodes.label: Paris
nodes.description: Became the center of French royal coinage for centuries, home to the Monnaie de Paris and the BnF's coin collection.

nodes.label: Barcelona
nodes.description: Carolingian monogram coinage circulated as far as Northeast Spain, showing the reach of the reform.

nodes.label: Rome
nodes.description: Charlemagne modeled his imperial coin portraits on Roman precedent, tying his currency to the legacy of empire.

nodes.label: Frankfurt (Modern Eurozone)
nodes.description: Centuries later, the franc's story would end here — at the European Central Bank, when the franc was retired for the euro in 2002.

historical_map_source.title: Encyclopaedia Britannica — Charlemagne and the Carolingian Coinages
